icd_category: T43.595A — Adverse effect of other antipsychotics and neuroleptics, initial encounter
one_liner: A 58-year-old man with bipolar I disorder on chronic lithium therapy presents with coarse tremor, vomiting, confusion, and ataxia after starting an ACE inhibitor for new-onset hypertension.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient has been stable on lithium 900 mg twice daily for 12 years for bipolar I disorder. Two weeks ago, his primary care physician started lisinopril 20 mg daily for newly diagnosed hypertension. Over the past 3 days, he has developed progressively worsening nausea, vomiting (4-5 episodes per day), coarse bilateral hand tremor, unsteady gait, slurred speech, and confusion. His wife reports he has been increasingly drowsy and 'not himself' — forgetting recent conversations and appearing disoriented. She also noticed him stumbling into walls. He has had decreased oral intake due to nausea. He has had no fever, seizures, or recent illness. No changes to diet or fluid intake prior to symptom onset. He has not started any NSAIDs or diuretics.
stage2.pmh: Bipolar I disorder (diagnosed age 28, well-controlled on lithium). Hypertension (newly diagnosed 2 weeks ago). Hypothyroidism (lithium-induced, on levothyroxine).
stage2.medications: Lithium carbonate 900 mg BID, levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily (started 2 weeks ago)
stage2.family_history: Father with bipolar disorder. Mother with hypertension and chronic kidney disease. Brother with alcohol use disorder.
stage2.social_history: Retired electrician. Married for 30 years. Lives with wife. One adult child. Drinks 1-2 beers per week. Denies tobacco or illicit drug use. No recent travel or illness exposure.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Drowsy, ill-appearing man, responds slowly to questions**. HEENT: **Dry mucous membranes**; **Pupils equal and reactive but sluggish**. Neck: **Mild thyromegaly**. Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, mild tachycardia, no murmurs. Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender diffusely, normoactive bowel sounds. Extremities: No edema, decreased skin turgor; **Neurological: Coarse bilateral tremor (worse than baseline per wife), dysmetria on finger-to-nose, wide-based ataxic gait, hyperreflexia (3+) in bilateral lower extremities**; **Mental Status Exam: Appearance — disheveled, clothing stained**; **Behavior — cooperative but slow**; **Speech — slurred, slow rate**; **Mood — 'I feel sick**; **' Affect — blunted**; **Thought process — impoverished, tangential at times**; **Thought content — no delusions, no SI/HI**; **Perception — no hallucinations**; Cognition — oriented to person only, unable to perform serial 7s, impaired short-term recall (0/3 at 5 minutes); **Insight — poor**; **Judgment — impaired**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Severely elevated lithium level (therapeutic range 0.6-1.2 mEq/L) confirming this condition

lab_results.result: Na 148, K 3.2, Cl 112, CO2 18, BUN 42, Cr 2.8, Glucose 105
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hypernatremia, hypokalemia, metabolic acidosis, and acute kidney injury — dehydration from vomiting and ACE inhibitor-induced renal impairment contributing to lithium accumulation
Leukocytosis (lithium-induced) and hemoconcentration consistent with dehydration
Mildly elevated TSH — chronic lithium use can worsen hypothyroidism; levothyroxine may need adjustment
Hypercalcemia — lithium can cause hyperparathyroidism; may contribute to symptoms
Hypomagnesemia likely from vomiting and poor oral intake
Low phosphorus consistent with metabolic derangement

lab_results.result: Specific gravity 1.030, no WBC, no bacteria, no casts
lab_results.clinical_significance: Concentrated urine consistent with dehydration and prerenal azotemia

lab_results.result: Sinus tachycardia at 94 bpm, flattened T waves, prolonged QTc at 480 ms, no ST changes
lab_results.clinical_significance: ECG changes consistent with the suspected diagnosis and electrolyte abnormalities — QTc prolongation requires monitoring

imaging_results.result: No acute intracranial abnormality. No mass, hemorrhage, or hydrocephalus. Mild generalized cerebral atrophy appropriate for age.
Clear lung fields. Normal cardiac silhouette.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: No evidence of aspiration pneumonia despite vomiting

key_tests: BMP — revealing AKI (Cr 2.8), hypernatremia, hypokalemia, and metabolic acidosis
ECG — showing QTc prolongation and T-wave flattening
Medication reconciliation — identifying recent addition of ACE inhibitor as precipitant
acceptable_tests: CBC — baseline and to assess for hemoconcentration
Urinalysis — assessing hydration status and renal function
low_yield_tests: Lumbar puncture (no meningeal signs or fever)

stage5.final_clue: The patient's serum lithium level is critically elevated at 3.

debrief.classic_presentation: A patient on chronic lithium therapy presenting with the progression from mild toxicity (fine tremor, GI upset, polyuria) to moderate toxicity (coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, slurred speech) to severe toxicity (seizures, coma, renal failure). A common precipitant is the addition of a medication that impairs renal lithium clearance (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, NSAIDs, thiazide diuretics) or volume depletion from any cause.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: The distinction between chronic lithium toxicity and acute ingestion is clinically critical. In chronic toxicity (as in this case), CNS and renal manifestations predominate because lithium has had time to equilibrate into tissues — serum levels may underestimate tissue burden. In acute ingestion, GI symptoms dominate early while CNS is relatively spared. Hemodialysis is indicated for levels >4.0 in acute ingestion or >2.5 in chronic toxicity with significant symptoms, or any patient with AKI preventing clearance. The EXTRIP guidelines provide specific indications. Notably, lithium has a rebound phenomenon post-dialysis as tissue stores re-equilibrate, so serial levels and potentially repeated dialysis sessions are necessary. The nephrogenic diabetes insipidus from chronic lithium use may paradoxically worsen dehydration and toxicity.
debrief.pathophysiology: Lithium is a monovalent cation with a narrow therapeutic index (0.6-1.2 mEq/L). It is freely filtered at the glomerulus and approximately 80% is reabsorbed in the proximal tubule via sodium channels. Any condition that increases proximal tubule sodium reabsorption (volume depletion, decreased GFR, sodium restriction) will proportionally increase lithium reabsorption. ACE inhibitors decrease GFR and enhance proximal tubule sodium reabsorption, directly reducing lithium clearance. At toxic levels, lithium interferes with inositol phosphate signaling, sodium-potassium ATPase, and multiple neurotransmitter systems, causing neurotoxicity. Chronic lithium exposure also causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (via downregulation of aquaporin-2) and hyperparathyroidism.
debrief.treatment_overview: Immediate management: discontinue lithium and the offending agent (ACE inhibitor). Aggressive IV normal saline resuscitation to restore volume and enhance renal lithium clearance. Correct electrolyte abnormalities (potassium, magnesium). Continuous cardiac monitoring given QTc prolongation. For severe toxicity (level >2.5 with symptoms, or >4.0 regardless): emergent hemodialysis is the definitive treatment — lithium is ideally suited for dialysis (small molecule, water-soluble, no protein binding, low volume of distribution). Monitor lithium levels every 2-4 hours post-dialysis for rebound. Avoid whole bowel irrigation (only useful in acute ingestion). Long-term: reassess need for lithium vs alternative mood stabilizer, consider renal function trajectory.
high_yield_pearls: The mnemonic for drugs that increase lithium levels: 'LITHIUM' — Losartan/ACE inhibitors, Ibuprofen/NSAIDs, Thiazides, Haldol (minimal), Inadequate sodium/dehydration, Uncompensated renal failure, Metronidazole.
Lithium therapeutic range is narrow (0.6-1.2 mEq/L); toxicity symptoms begin at 1.5 mEq/L: mild (1.5-2.0), moderate (2.0-2.5), severe (>2.5).
ACE inhibitors, ARBs, NSAIDs, and thiazide diuretics are the most common medications precipitating lithium toxicity — always check drug interactions before prescribing.
Chronic lithium toxicity has worse neurological outcomes than acute ingestion at the same serum level because of greater CNS tissue accumulation over time.

Hemodialysis indications (EXTRIP guidelines): lithium >4.0 in acute, >2.5 in chronic with significant symptoms, impaired renal function preventing clearance, or life-threatening symptoms regardless of level.
Monitor for rebound in lithium levels 6-12 hours post-dialysis due to redistribution from tissue compartments — repeat dialysis may be needed.
sources: Decker BS et al. Extracorporeal treatment for lithium poisoning: systematic review and recommendations from the EXTRIP workgroup. Clin J Am Soc Nephrol, 2015
Kaplan & Sadock's Comprehensive Textbook of Psychiatry, 10th Edition
Timmer RT, Sands JM. Lithium intoxication. J Am Soc Nephrol, 1999
Gitlin M. Lithium side effects and toxicity: prevalence and management strategies. Int J Bipolar Disord, 2016

after_stage2_more_likely: Lithium toxicity — chronic lithium use with recent ACE inhibitor addition, classic neurotoxic triad (tremor, ataxia, confusion), dehydration
Uremic encephalopathy — dry mucous membranes, vomiting, altered mental status
after_stage2_less_likely: Serotonin syndrome — no serotonergic medications, no clonus, fever, or diaphoresis
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome — no antipsychotic use, no rigidity or hyperthermia
Stroke — gradual onset over days rather than acute
after_labs_top3: Lithium toxicity — critically elevated lithium level (3.2 mEq/L) with AKI precipitated by ACE inhibitor, classic neurotoxic presentation
Acute kidney injury with metabolic encephalopathy — contributing factor but secondary to lithium toxicity and dehydration
Lithium-induced hyperparathyroidism with hypercalcemia — calcium 11.2, contributing to confusion but not the primary diagnosis
